seasonal_wine.name: Saint-Vincent Tournante, late January
seasonal_wine.description: The winegrowers' year opens with the Saint-Vincent Tournante, a two-day festival in honour of the patron saint of vine-growers held each late January in a different Burgundian village. Cellars open for tasting kits and the host village dresses its streets in paper flowers.
what_to_taste: Village-level Bourgogne reds and whites from the host village
seasonal_wine.tip: The location rotates each year, so confirm the host village before booking; 2026 lands in the Maranges at the southern tip of the Cote de Beaune.

seasonal_wine.name: Winter pruning across the Cote d'Or, January to February
seasonal_wine.description: The deep winter is the pruning season. Across the Cote de Nuits and Cote de Beaune growers walk the rows cutting back canes in the cold, the quiet work that sets each vine's crop for the coming vintage. Cellars are calm and tastings unhurried.
seasonal_wine.tip: Winter is the best time for an unrushed domaine appointment; growers have more time between pruning days and many cellars are at their quietest.

seasonal_wine.name: Grands Jours de Bourgogne, March of even years
seasonal_wine.description: Every second March the entire region opens for the Grands Jours de Bourgogne, a five-day professional tasting marathon from Chablis to the Maconnais. The 2026 edition runs 9 to 13 March; producers along the route frequently open for visits the same week.
what_to_taste: Latest-vintage Grand Cru and Premier Cru releases
seasonal_wine.tip: The walk-around days are trade-only, but the buzz spills into the villages; time a non-trade March visit to this week for the most active cellars of the year.

seasonal_wine.name: Budbreak and frost watch, April
seasonal_wine.description: Budbreak arrives in April and with it the threat of spring frost, a recurring danger on the Cote d'Or that has cut yields hard in recent vintages. Growers light candles and run wind machines through cold dawns to protect the fragile new shoots.
what_to_taste: Cremant de Bourgogne as an aperitif
seasonal_wine.tip: April is shoulder season with light crowds; ask growers about their frost-protection set-up, a vivid window into how fragile a Burgundian crop can be.

seasonal_wine.name: Flowering and fruit set, June
seasonal_wine.description: Flowering through June fixes the size of the crop, with roughly a hundred days from flowering to harvest a rule of thumb on the Cote d'Or. Warm settled weather at flowering points to a generous vintage; rain and coulure thin the bunches.
what_to_taste: Chilled village whites in the vineyard
seasonal_wine.tip: Count roughly a hundred days from flowering to estimate harvest; a mid-June flowering usually means a late-September pick.

seasonal_wine.name: Peak-season cellar visits, July to August
seasonal_wine.description: High summer is the busiest visitor window on the Route des Grands Crus from Dijon to Santenay. The vines are in full leaf, the Cite des Climats centres run tastings, and Beaune's courtyards fill, though many small domaines slow down for August holidays.
what_to_taste: Route des Grands Crus tasting flights
Cremant de Bourgogne and white Burgundy
seasonal_wine.tip: Book small-domaine appointments well ahead in summer and expect some August closures; the larger Beaune maisons and the Cite des Climats stay open through the holidays.

seasonal_wine.name: Veraison, late August
seasonal_wine.description: In late August the Pinot Noir berries colour up at veraison and the Chardonnay softens, the last stretch before harvest. Growers walk the rows tasting for ripeness and removing green bunches, fine-tuning the picking date.
what_to_taste: Estate barrel samples of the prior vintage
seasonal_wine.tip: Late August is the calm before harvest; ask whether the estate green-harvests, a telling sign of how it manages ripeness in warm vintages.

seasonal_wine.name: Vendange on the Cote d'Or, September
seasonal_wine.description: Harvest sweeps the Cote d'Or in September, whites first in the Cote de Beaune then Pinot Noir across the Cote de Nuits. Recent warm vintages have pushed picking earlier, sometimes into late August, and the villages buzz with pickers and crushing must.
what_to_taste: Cellar-side fermenting must on private visits
seasonal_wine.tip: Harvest dates move year to year, so confirm with the estate before travelling; midweek visits avoid the weekend crush and give the best chance of seeing pickers in the rows.

seasonal_wine.name: Harvest in Chablis and the Maconnais, September
seasonal_wine.description: Chablis in the north and the Maconnais in the south pick their Chardonnay through September. Cool northern Chablis often finishes a touch later than the warmer Maconnais, where Pouilly-Fuisse and Saint-Veran fruit comes in under the Roche de Solutre.
what_to_taste: Chablis Premier Cru from cooler vintages
seasonal_wine.tip: A harvest-time loop from Chablis down to the Maconnais shows the full north-to-south range of white Burgundy in a single trip.

seasonal_wine.name: Fete des vins de Chablis, late October
seasonal_wine.description: On the fourth weekend of October the town of Chablis baptises its new vintage. Around 40 producers of Chablis and the Grand Auxerrois pour every tier from Petit Chablis to Grand Cru, alongside a brotherhood induction and walks through the vines.
seasonal_wine.tip: This is the easiest day of the year to taste the full Chablis hierarchy side by side; climb the Grand Cru slope above town the same morning.

seasonal_wine.name: Les Trois Glorieuses and the Hospices auction, November
seasonal_wine.description: The third weekend of November is the climax of the Burgundian calendar: the Saturday Tastevin banquet at Clos de Vougeot, the Sunday Hospices de Beaune charity auction in the Halles de Beaune, and the Monday Paulee de Meursault harvest lunch.
what_to_taste: Older white Burgundy poured at the Paulee
Cote de Nuits Grand Cru at the Tastevin banquet
seasonal_wine.tip: The whole Cote d'Or books out for this weekend; reserve Beaune or Meursault lodging a year ahead and catch the free Festivinales tastings around the auction.

seasonal_wine.name: Beaujolais Nouveau release, third Thursday of November
seasonal_wine.description: Just south of Burgundy proper, the Gamay-based Beaujolais Nouveau is released by law on the third Thursday of November. The same week as the Hospices auction, it is the most visible new-vintage event in greater Burgundy, a young carbonic-maceration red drunk within months.
what_to_taste: Cru Beaujolais such as Morgon and Fleurie
seasonal_wine.tip: Treat Nouveau as a fun first taste of the vintage's Gamay, then trade up to a Cru Beaujolais from Morgon or Moulin-a-Vent for a wine built to age.
